game_name: Desert Strike: Return to the Gulf

danger_signals.visual_form: Grounded weapon emplacements visible on isometric map
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat zones; player must destroy or avoid

danger_signals.signal: Scud launchers and chemical weapons facilities
danger_signals.visual_form: Distinctive military structures marked on terrain

danger_signals.meaning: Active aerial combat threat requiring evasion or destruction
Cumulative damage; zero armor results in craft destruction

danger_signals.visual_form: Fuel meter on HUD progressively emptying
danger_signals.meaning: Resource scarcity danger; craft crashes if depleted

danger_signals.visual_form: Ground-based vehicle sprites with weapon hardpoints

safety_signals.meaning: Safe extraction point for hostages/prisoners; healing via rescued personnel

safety_signals.visual_form: Collectible power-up sprites distinct from fuel
Health recovery mechanic tied to NPC pickup
safety_signals.meaning: Humanitarian objective doubles as survival resource

safety_signals.visual_form: Uncluttered map areas in isometric view

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Landing zones, American embassy locations, rescue objectives marked as allied

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Military installations, dictator's private yacht (final stage)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Enemy soldiers, anti-aircraft weapons, tanks, armored vehicles, scud launchers, chemical/biological weapons facilities
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fortified positions, airstrips, weapons plants, nuclear facilities, garbage trucks with nuclear material (obfuscation tactic)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Hostages, P.O.W.s, U.N. inspection teams, embassy personnel
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Rescue mission locations; visual distinction from military combatants

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy airstrips and support facilities visible as destructible installations on open desert terrain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Pro-American spy isolated and vulnerable; requires player extraction

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mission 2 (Scud Buster) - Chemical weapons proliferation threat
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Chemical weapons plant and scud launcher emplacements displayed on isometric map
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Non-combatants and P.O.W.s scattered; require evacuation before plant destruction

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Mission 3 (Embassy City) - Urban conflict with diplomatic stakes
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: U.N. inspection team, American embassy, biological weapons facility, and Kilbaba's yacht marked as distinct structures
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Large hostage contingent (embassy personnel); Kilbaba flees when cornered on yacht, escalating threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Nuclear power plant, weapons components, oil facility, and garbage trucks (nuclear material disguised) visible across terrain
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Kilbaba has anticipated final strike; nuclear material distribution indicates desperation defense

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Finite fuel and ammo indicators on HUD; power-up placement requires route optimization
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must plan mission paths to intercept fuel/ammo before depletion; creates risk-reward tension

environmental_storytelling.example: Airstrips → chemical plants → biological facilities → nuclear installations; escalating strategic threat level communicated through facility complexity
Scattered rescue objectives communicate Kilbaba's civilian hostage strategy and U.S. humanitarian intervention mandate
Private yacht and fortified positions mark Kilbaba's regime; final location (nuclear-equipped yacht) signals ultimate threat concentration
Open desert with scattered fuel/ammo crates and landing zones communicates logistical challenge; no safe bases, player must improvise supply lines
Chemical, biological, and nuclear plants grouped in later missions; spatial density indicates escalating WMD development
Nuclear material hidden in civilian vehicles; visual deception communicates regime cunning and final desperation tactic

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Full offensive capability; aggressive patrolling and targeting optimal
Destroyed; mission restart; communicates vulnerability to attrition
Forced to seek fuel barrels or landing zone; restricted movement radius
Active engagement of player; requires evasion or air-to-air combat
Automated fire on player proximity; creates danger zones on map
Ground-based threats with sustained firepower; slower than helicopter but heavily armored
Stationary at locations; indicate incomplete objectives
Heal player armor upon delivery; communicate mission progress
Flees when cornered on yacht; signals evasion and continued threat escalation
Heavily defended with nuclear arsenal; represents existential finale

notes: Desert Strike communicates world-state primarily through isometric map layout and mission structure progression. Danger is signaled via destructible target placement, resource scarcity mechanics (fuel/ammo), and escalating facility types (conventional → WMD). Factions are identified implicitly: U.S. through Apache identity and rescue objectives; Kilbaba's regime through fortified installations and WMD development. Political narrative (Gulf War-inspired conflict with dictator, humanitarian rescue mandate, nuclear proliferation threat) is embedded in mission objectives and environmental placement rather than explicit UI messaging. The game uses resource management as a manifestation layer—limited fuel and ammunition force player route optimization, communicating logistical constraints of air-strike operations. Civilian hostages scattered across missions reinforce U.S. intervention legitimacy. Kilbaba's yacht escape and nuclear material disguise in garbage trucks communicate regime desperation in endgame.
